skill.name: gh-ticket
skill.description: Create or update detailed GitHub issues with full context, requirements, acceptance criteria, implementation notes, and related links.
skill.summary: Turn investigation context into complete GitHub issue specifications.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, browser storage, reconnaissance, and weak-crypto patterns. Manual review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, credential theft, or real cryptographic code, but the skill does instruct assistants to run local shell and authenticated GitHub CLI commands. Publish with a warning that users should review commands and issue content before execution.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitHub CLI Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs assistants to create, edit, view, and comment on GitHub issues with gh commands. This is core functionality, but it can publish sensitive context or modify repository issues if the user does not review the generated body first.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically tied to authenticated GitHub issue changes. The risk is legitimate but appears user-directed rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Discovery Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends git log, grep, find, and xargs commands to gather technical evidence. These commands are normal for issue preparation, but they can expose local filenames, repository history, and matched documentation content in issue text.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact commands are present in bash examples and are intended to inspect the local repository. No destructive action is shown, but captured output could include sensitive project context.

low_findings.title: Temporary Ticket Body File
low_findings.description: The skill shows writing ticket content to /tmp/ticket.md before creating an issue. This is a common workflow, but temporary files can retain sensitive issue drafts on shared systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary file write is explicit and used for GitHub CLI input. The risk is limited because the file path is fixed and the example does not read secrets or execute the file.

low_findings.title: Static High-Severity Labels Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography, browser-storage, and reconnaissance labels point to markdown prose, placeholders, Mermaid examples, or sample requirements rather than executable code. No evidence found of malicious crypto use, browser storage access, or network scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual line review shows these flagged terms occur in documentation examples. They do not invoke APIs or implement behavior by themselves.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in GitHub CLI Body
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses command substitution with heredocs to pass generated bodies into gh issue create, edit, and comment commands. This can be safe with quoted heredocs, but review is needed before publishing user or repository context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution pattern is directly present. The quoted EOF marker reduces interpolation risk inside the body, but the outer shell still executes gh commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: xargs-Based Repository Search
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill suggests find piped to xargs grep for related documentation. This can traverse many markdown files and may include sensitive matches in generated tickets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The xargs command is explicit and performs repository-wide searching. It is not malicious, but broad output capture can leak internal details into issue content.

content.value_statement: Important implementation context is often lost before work starts. This skill turns current findings into clear GitHub issues with requirements, acceptance criteria, and implementation notes.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of detailed GitHub issue bodies from current investigation context.
Structures requirements, edge cases, acceptance criteria, and out-of-scope notes.
Adds implementation guidance with affected files, code locations, dependencies, and blockers.
Explains when to update an issue body versus adding a comment.
Provides examples for bug reports, feature requests, and Mermaid diagrams.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI access for direct issue creation or updates.
Does not verify that captured requirements are technically correct.
Can include sensitive repository context if users do not review issue text.
Does not automate prioritization, assignment, or sprint planning.

use_cases.title: Convert Investigation Notes Into a Bug Ticket
use_cases.description: Capture symptoms, current behavior, expected behavior, affected files, and acceptance checks before debugging context is lost.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Feature Specification for Implementation
use_cases.description: Turn a product request into a GitHub issue with functional requirements, edge cases, dependencies, and clear scope boundaries.

use_cases.title: Improve Existing Vague Issues
use_cases.description: Rewrite or comment on existing issues with missing context, better acceptance criteria, and concrete implementation notes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Bug Ticket
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner ticket creation after finding a bug.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub issue for this bug. Include the current behavior, expected behavior, reproduction notes, affected files, and acceptance criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Feature Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a complete GitHub issue for this feature request. Separate functional requirements, edge cases, out-of-scope items, dependencies, and implementation notes.

prompt_templates.title: Update an Existing Issue
prompt_templates.scenario: New context was discovered during investigation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update issue number [number] with the new context I found. Preserve useful existing details and add requirements, code locations, risks, and acceptance checks.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Implementation-Ready Ticket
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an implementation-ready GitHub issue from this investigation. Include related issues, affected files, suggested approach, validation plan, Mermaid diagrams if useful, and explicit non-goals.

output_examples.input: A user reports crashes when uploading files larger than 10 MB.
output: A bug issue with summary, background, current and desired behavior.
Checklist requirements for client validation, server validation, clear error messaging, and logging.
Acceptance criteria covering oversized, exact-limit, and normal uploads.

output_examples.input: A team wants dark mode with system preference support.
output: A feature issue with user-facing goals and persistence requirements.
Implementation notes for theme state, settings UI, and preference storage.
Out-of-scope notes for custom themes and scheduling.

output_examples.input: An existing issue says only: Fix authentication.
output: A rewritten issue that defines the failing auth case, expected response, affected files, and test plan.
A decision on whether to replace the body or add a comment based on the scope of changes.

best_practices: Review generated issue text before publishing it to GitHub.
Include exact file paths, line references, errors, and related links when available.
Write each requirement as a separate testable checkbox.
anti_patterns: Do not create issues with vague titles or missing current-state context.
Do not publish secrets, private customer data, or sensitive logs in issue bodies.
Do not mix unrelated features, fixes, and refactors in one ticket.

faq.question: Does this skill create GitHub issues automatically?
faq.answer: It provides commands and structure for GitHub CLI workflows. Users should review every command and issue body before execution.

faq.question: Can it update existing issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when to replace an issue body, update a title, add labels, or add a comment with new context.

faq.question: Does it require the GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Direct creation and updates use the GitHub CLI. The writing guidance can still be used without running CLI commands.

faq.question: What makes the generated tickets implementation-ready?
faq.answer: They include background, desired behavior, requirements, edge cases, acceptance criteria, affected files, dependencies, and out-of-scope notes.

faq.question: Can it include diagrams?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends focused Mermaid diagrams for flows, state changes, component relationships, and timelines.

faq.question: What should users check before publishing?
faq.answer: Users should remove secrets, private logs, unrelated context, and speculative requirements before creating or updating an issue.
